# tokens: # word / __SP splice marker
# `word <value>` wraps its (unevaluated) argument in an internal __SP
# splice marker. When the marker reaches the stack pointer it is
# replaced by its payload and re-stepped against the LIVE stack:
#   - a plain list contributes its top-level elements (so a word-bearing
#     list runs as a Forth-style macro),
#   - any other value contributes itself.
# `def name word value` binds the marker, giving an explicit splice
# (the type-agnostic counterpart of the implicit `def name [list]`
# splice, which is unchanged). Expected values use the canon renderer
# (containers are space-separated).
# 1. Standalone `word`: scalar splices itself, list splices its elements
word 42
note: a scalar splices as a single value
a plain list splices its top-level elements

# tokens: word [] 9

note: only the top level splices; inner lists stay values

# tokens: # 2. Macro: spliced words execute against the live stack
5 word [dup add]
note: dup add run against the caller's 5 -> 10

# tokens: 1 2 word [add] 10
# 3. def name word value — binds the splice marker
# FORWARD form only. `word`'s __SP must be collected as def's forward
# argument (the pointer skips a collected arg, so the marker does not
# fire). There is NO prefix-form splice — `[body] def name` puts the
# body on the stack BEFORE def, so the pointer steps (and fires) any
# marker before def consumes it; a prefix list body therefore binds the
# list VALUE. `quote` can't ferry the marker either: `quote (word …)`
# fires the __SP inside the paren before quote marks it, and `quote
# word …` captures the literal token `word` as an atom. Use the forward
# form below for splices; use prefix only for value binding.
def doub word [dup add] 5 doub
note: explicit splice-macro (cf. implicit def doub [dup add])

# tokens: def xs word [1,2,3] [xs]
note: re-collecting the reference rebuilds the list

# tokens: def n word 42 n add 1
note: scalar splice binds a single value

# tokens: def p word [1 add 2] [p]
note: value stored raw; `1 add 2` runs when spliced+evaluated

# tokens: # 4. Splices inside containers when the container is evaluated
def xs word [1,2,3] [0 xs 4]
note: a reference splices inside a list literal

# tokens: def a word [1,2] def b word [a 3] [b]
def e word [] [e 9]
note: empty splice contributes nothing inside a container

# tokens: # 5. Negative: the splice is unevaluated but the tokens are real code
word [dup]
note: spliced `dup` runs on an empty stack -> no matching signature

# tokens: word
# 6. `def name [list]` now binds the evaluated list VALUE (like a map)
# The implicit list-def splice was removed; `word` is the explicit splice.
def xs [1,2,3] length xs
note: a def'd list is a value, not a splice

# tokens: def xs [1 add 2] xs
note: the list is evaluated at def time (like a map)

# tokens: length [1,2,3]
[1 add 2]
